title: FOUNDATIONS - Lesson 9: Soteriology - Repentance and Faith
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: FOUNDATIONS - Lesson 9 - Soteriology_ Salvation - Repentance and Faith - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Review: Regeneration Preceding Faith
subpoints: Last week: God alone effects regeneration; the person is completely passive, as dead men are
Regeneration accomplishes: enlightening of the mind (Acts 26:17-18), renewing of the heart (Ezekiel 36:26-27), and a new will that is now oriented toward God
1 John 5:1: whoever believes has already been born - the new birth precedes and enables belief
The struggle with sin in a believer is the remnant of the old nature, not the core of the new nature

outline.title: Repentance Unto Life: Definition and Scope
subpoints: Repentance unto life is that initial radical and conscious change of opinion, feeling, and purpose with respect to God, ourselves, sin, and righteousness
It affects the three elements of personhood: intellect (opinion), emotions (feeling), and will (purpose)
Repentance unto life is repenting of being a sinner, not enumerating every sin committed before conversion
It is not the Reformation of life before believing; it acknowledges our inability to reform our own lives

outline.title: Repentance as Essential to Gospel Proclamation
subpoints: Jesus' very first recorded sermon: 'Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand' (Matthew 4:17)
Jesus came to call sinners to repentance (Matthew 9:13); this was the center of His ministry
Paul's summary of his ministry in Acts 20:21: testifying of repentance toward God and faith in the Lord Jesus Christ
It is a grievous sin for a minister of the gospel not to call people to repent of sin; without this call the gospel proclamation is incomplete

outline.title: Saving Faith: Knowledge, Assent, and Trust
subpoints: Saving faith has three elements: notitia (knowledge of the gospel), assensus (assent that the knowledge is true and applies to me), and fiducia (trust - committing oneself to Christ)
Faith is not a leap in the dark (contra Kierkegaard); it is grounded in knowledge and intellectual conviction
Faith comes by hearing, and hearing by the Word of God (Romans 10:17); one must know the gospel to believe it
Saving faith is a gift from God, not a human achievement: 'that' in Ephesians 2:8 refers back to the entire clause including the faith (Philippians 1:29)

outline.title: Saving Faith vs. Law-Keeping
subpoints: Saving faith is diametrically opposed to law-keeping; Romans 3:20 - by deeds of the law no flesh shall be justified
Romans 3:21-22: the righteousness of God apart from the law is revealed - through faith in Jesus Christ
Romans 4:5: to him who does not work but believes on Him who justifies the ungodly, his faith is counted for righteousness
Galatians 3 - the entire argument for salvation hangs on the word 'seed' (singular) vs. 'seeds' (plural)

summary:
This session addresses the final two steps in the order of salvation that occur at conversion: repentance unto life and faith in Jesus Christ. Pastor Lyro opens with a review of last week's teaching on regeneration - that God alone effects the new birth while the person is entirely passive - and moves now to the human response that follows: repentance and faith. These are synergistic steps where both God acts and the human responds, though ultimately still the result of God's grace.

Repentance unto life is defined as that initial, radical, and conscious change of opinion (intellect), feeling (emotions), and purpose (will) with respect to God, oneself, sin, and righteousness. It is not the enumeration of every past sin, nor is it the reformation of life prior to believing. It is rather the acknowledgment that one is a sinner who cannot reform oneself - a repenting of being a sinner. Lyro argues that calling people to repentance is not optional for faithful gospel proclamation: Jesus' first sermon was 'Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand,' and Paul summarized his entire ministry as 'testifying of repentance toward God and faith in the Lord Jesus Christ' (Acts 20:21). A minister who never calls people to repent has departed from the gospel.

The session then addresses saving faith, which consists of three inseparable elements: notitia (knowledge of the gospel), assensus (intellectual conviction that the gospel is true and true for me), and fiducia (personal trust and commitment to Christ). Faith is not Kierkegaard's leap in the dark but a reasonable and knowledge-grounded act. Ephesians 2:8-9 teaches that even the faith itself is a gift from God - the word 'that' refers back to the entire clause 'for by grace you have been saved through faith,' and Philippians 1:29 confirms that faith is granted, not generated by human will.

The session closes with a careful distinction between saving faith and law-keeping. Romans 3 and 4 demonstrate categorically that justification is by faith apart from the deeds of the law, and Paul's argument in Galatians 3 even hangs on the singular 'seed' versus the plural 'seeds' - showing that every word of Scripture matters.
transcript:
## Introduction

We continue our series on foundations — talking about salvation and the doctrine of soteriology. This is the last part of our series, and we are looking at ten different categories under the idea of salvation.

The main point to hold onto: God alone saves, and He saves through the proclamation of the gospel. His salvation starts with the new birth and concludes with our glorification.

Last week we looked at effectual calling and regeneration. We saw that in giving a person the new birth, God changes his or her nature and enables that person to believe the gospel. Regeneration is entirely of God — man is completely passive in it. When God begins the work of regeneration, the person contributes nothing. John 6:37 says: "All that the Father gives Me will come to Me." The Father initiates. The Father gives people to the Son. And later in the same chapter: "No one can come to Me unless the Father who sent Me draws him" (John 6:44).

We also saw that regeneration accomplishes three things: it enlightens the mind (making it able to understand the gospel), it renews and purifies the nature of the heart (the core promise of the new covenant — God will give us a new heart), and because the nature changes, the will is freed to do what God calls us to do.

Today we are going to look at the next two steps: repentance unto life and faith in Jesus Christ.

## Repentance Unto Life

Repentance unto life is the first sign of regeneration — and it is synergistic, meaning we are doing something, though the whole thing is still the result of God's grace.

The definition: repentance unto life is that initial, radical, and conscious change of opinion — involving the intellect, emotions, and will — with respect to God, ourselves, sin, and righteousness. It is a change of intellect (the mind), emotions (the heart), and will (the purpose). These three things describe all of personhood. So repentance unto life affects every element of who you are.

In repentance unto life we acknowledge that we are sinners and that our sins entail personal guilt, defilement, and helplessness before God. We sorrow with a godly sorrow for the sins we have committed against the holy and just God. And we resolve to seek pardon and cleansing from God through the blood of Jesus Christ, which satisfied the offended God.

Repentance unto life happens once in our lives — it is that first repentance when we come to faith in Jesus Christ. In essence, repentance unto life is repenting of being a sinner. Some people think they must repent of every sin they have ever committed before coming to faith. That is not what the Bible calls us to. It is covered in the essence of repentance of being a sinner — that one's nature has been changed.

## Repentance Is Central to Gospel Proclamation

Repentance unto life is a necessary part of the proclamation of the gospel alongside faith in Jesus Christ. You are not proclaiming the gospel if you are not calling people to repent. Repentance is the basic message of Jesus's preaching. His very first recorded sermon: "Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand" (Matthew 4:17).

Matthew 9:10–13:

> *"Now it happened, as Jesus sat at the table in the house, that behold, many tax collectors and sinners came and sat down with Him and His disciples. And when the Pharisees saw it, they said to His disciples, 'Why does your Teacher eat with tax collectors and sinners?' When Jesus heard that, He said to them, 'Those who are well have no need of a physician, but those who are sick. But go and learn what this means: "I desire mercy and not sacrifice." For I did not come to call the righteous, but sinners, to repentance.'"* (Matthew 9:10–13)

He says: this is why I'm here — to call sinners to repent. You cannot have a proclamation of the gospel without that call.

Paul summarizes his own preaching to the Ephesian elders in Acts 20: "testifying to Jews, and also to Greeks, repentance toward God and faith toward our Lord Jesus Christ" (Acts 20:21). That was the content of his testimony — repentance toward God. It is a grievous sin for a minister of the gospel not to call people to repent.

## Repentance Is a Gift, Not a Work

Repentance unto life is not reformation of life before believing in Jesus. It is not changing your life so that you conform to Scripture before placing faith in Jesus. Rather, it acknowledges our inability to reform our lives. It is not necessarily the immediate cessation of a particular sin either. Someone may come to genuine faith in Jesus Christ, truly repent of being a sinner, and yet still struggle with a particular sin. Repentance unto life is the initial, radical change of orientation — not the instant elimination of all sinful behavior.

Repentance unto life is the result of Christ's work of redemption and is produced by regeneration. Only those who have been born again will repent. Luke 24:46–48:

> *"Then He said to them, 'Thus it is written, and thus it was necessary for the Christ to suffer and to rise from the dead the third day, and that repentance and remission of sins should be preached in His name to all nations, beginning at Jerusalem. And you are witnesses of these things.'"* (Luke 24:46–48)

Christ came to purchase this repentance. Paul says in 2 Timothy 2:25 that God may grant them repentance. Repentance is granted by God through the new birth.

## Faith in Jesus Christ

The other side of the coin is faith in Jesus Christ — saving faith. Ephesians 2:8–9:

> *"For by grace you have been saved through faith, and that not of yourselves; it is the gift of God, not of works, lest anyone should boast."* (Ephesians 2:8–9)

The grace, the salvation, the faith — all of it is the gift of God. Not of ourselves.

Saving faith has three elements. The first is knowledge. Kierkegaard famously spoke of faith as a leap in the dark — but that is not what the Bible teaches. Our faith is reasonable. Our faith is rich in knowledge. One must know the gospel. Romans 10:16–17:

> *"But they have not all obeyed the gospel. For Isaiah says, 'Lord, who has believed our report?' So then faith comes by hearing, and hearing by the word of God."* (Romans 10:16–17)

You cannot have faith in what you do not know. Knowledge is necessary.

The second element is assent — the intellectual and cognitive conviction that the knowledge one has acquired about Christ is indeed factually true and that the provisions of the gospel correspond exactly to one's actual needs. You hear the gospel, you know the gospel — and now you agree: this is true, and this is what I need.

The third element is trust — conviction passing to confidence. You know, you assent, and you embrace these things as your own. Robert Raymond: saving faith is when one cognitively, affectively, and volitionally transfers all reliance for pardon, righteousness, and cleansing away from himself and completely onto Christ, whom he joyfully receives and upon whom alone he rests entirely for his salvation.

When these three things operate together, we have saving faith in Jesus Christ.

## Saving Faith Is the Opposite of Law-Keeping

Saving faith is diametrically opposed to law-keeping. Even though God saves us because of our faith, saving faith is not law-keeping. It is not meritorious — there is nothing in it that earns us any merit before God. Romans 3:20–22:

> *"Therefore by the deeds of the law no flesh will be justified in His sight, for by the law is the knowledge of sin. But now the righteousness of God apart from the law is revealed, being witnessed by the Law and the Prophets, even the righteousness of God, through faith in Jesus Christ, to all and on all who believe."* (Romans 3:20–22)

Verse 28: "We conclude that a man is justified by faith apart from the deeds of the law." Romans 4:5: "But to him who does not work but believes on Him who justifies the ungodly, his faith is accounted for righteousness." The contrast throughout is between working and believing. They are opposite ends of the spectrum.

Faith itself is not of ourselves — it is the gift of God. So we cannot even boast of our faith. Paul says exactly that in Ephesians 2:9: "not of works, lest anyone should boast."

## Summary

God alone saves, and He saves through the proclamation of the gospel. His salvation starts with the new birth and concludes with our glorification. We have seen that those He effectually calls and regenerates respond — because of that regeneration — with repentance unto life and faith in Jesus Christ. Those born again repent of being sinners and turn to God in faith. Those born again exercise saving faith — knowledge, assent, and trust — in the Lord Jesus Christ. None of this is of ourselves. It is all of grace.
